message:
fix(close,search): partial-close requested dot-notation children + reject invalid --sort keys early

## close.rs: requested dot-notation children no longer block parent close

Previously, `br close bd-parent bd-parent.1` (parent + a legacy
dot-notation child) would skip the parent because the dot-notation
guard treated *all* open dot-notation children as blockers regardless
of whether the user had also requested them in the same invocation.

Now `execute_route` partitions open dot-notation children into two
sets:
  - `requested_children` — IDs the user passed in the same call
  - `unrequested_children` — IDs the user did *not* request

The skip-with-error path only fires when `unrequested_children` is
non-empty (and the error preview now lists those, not all). Requested
children are then folded into `blocker_ids` so the close engine still
processes them as part of the same close operation rather than
silently orphaning them.

Net effect: a user closing parent + child explicitly succeeds; a user
closing only the parent while children are open still gets the
\"use --force\" error, and the listed children in that error are now
the actual blockers instead of including the user's own requests.

Also adds `close_human_message` / `skipped_human_message` /
`unblocked_human_line` helpers that route ID, title, and reason
through `sanitize_terminal_inline`, so the operator's terminal can't
be hijacked by a closed issue's title (test:
`close_human_messages_sanitize_terminal_controls`).

New regression test
`execute_with_args_closes_requested_dot_notation_child_with_parent`
proves parent + dot child can be closed together.

## search.rs: validate --sort key before query

`build_filters` now calls `validate_sort_key` before constructing
ListFilters. The whitelist matches the SQL ORDER BY paths the storage
layer actually understands:
  priority, created_at, created, updated_at, updated, title

An unknown value (e.g. `--sort bogus`) returns
`BeadsError::Validation { field: \"sort\", reason: \"invalid sort
field 'bogus'\" }` immediately, instead of being silently dropped or
forwarded into the storage layer where behavior depended on whether
post-query reordering happened.

Test
`test_search_rejects_invalid_sort_without_client_filters` verifies
that even when no client-side filters force post-query ordering
(which previously would mask the issue), the invalid sort is rejected
with the structured error.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
